findings.title: No authentication mechanism defined for public-facing API endpoints
findings.description: The Equipment Catalog API (Task 2), Finance Service (Task 4), Customer Vetting Service (Task 5), and Social Media Engine (Task 6) all expose REST endpoints but have no explicit authentication implementation tasks. Decision point dp-6 for service-to-service auth remains unresolved, and dp-11 for admin endpoint access control is similarly open. Without authentication, all endpoints including admin operations (invoice creation, payroll processing, vetting runs, social media publishing) would be publicly accessible.
findings.recommendation: Add explicit tasks for: (1) Implementing an authentication middleware (JWT bearer validation or API key verification) on all service endpoints before any business logic handlers. (2) Resolving dp-6 immediately — recommend JWT-based service tokens for service-to-service and mTLS as a network-layer complement. (3) Resolving dp-11 — implement RBAC middleware that checks caller roles before allowing access to admin endpoints (finance, vetting, social publishing).

findings.title: Financial data (PCI-relevant) lacks encryption at rest and field-level protection
findings.description: The Finance Service (Task 4) stores Stripe payment intent IDs, invoice amounts, payroll records with employee compensation data, and potentially credit card metadata. Task 4001 defines database schema with NUMERIC columns but no mention of encryption at rest for the PostgreSQL database, column-level encryption for sensitive fields, or data masking for payroll/financial reports. The Stripe webhook secret and API keys are stored as Kubernetes secrets but there's no encryption verification for etcd at rest.
findings.recommendation: Add tasks for: (1) Enable PostgreSQL TDE (Transparent Data Encryption) or verify CloudNative-PG supports encrypted PVCs. (2) Implement column-level encryption using pgcrypto for PII fields (employee names, compensation amounts in payroll). (3) Verify Kubernetes etcd encryption is enabled for Secret resources. (4) Never store raw card data — ensure only Stripe tokens/IDs are persisted. (5) Add data masking for payroll report endpoints to redact individual compensation unless the caller has specific permissions.

findings.title: Customer PII in vetting service lacks data protection controls
findings.description: The Customer Vetting Service (Task 5) aggregates sensitive business intelligence: credit scores, incorporation data, LinkedIn profiles, and Google Reviews. This data is stored in the vetting_results table without mention of data retention policies, access controls on the stored data, or PII classification. Credit scoring data may be subject to regulations (FCRA in the US, PIPEDA in Canada) requiring specific handling.
findings.recommendation: Add tasks for: (1) Implement data retention policies — auto-delete or archive vetting results after a configurable period (e.g., 90 days). (2) Encrypt credit score data at the application level before storage. (3) Add access logging for all vetting result reads (who accessed which org's vetting data and when). (4) Consult legal/compliance on FCRA/PIPEDA obligations for storing and using credit data. (5) Implement data access audit trail in the audit schema.

findings.title: SQL injection risk in several services with raw query construction
findings.description: While sqlx (Rust services) and Drizzle ORM (Node.js) provide parameterized queries, several task descriptions mention constructing queries with user-supplied filters (category_id, search ILIKE, date ranges, barcode strings). Task 2003 specifically mentions 'ILIKE on name/description' and task 3006 mentions 'accept a barcode string, look up the inventory item'. Without explicit parameterized query enforcement and input sanitization validation tasks, there's risk of SQL injection if any raw query construction slips through.
findings.recommendation: Add an explicit task or checklist item for each service: (1) Mandate parameterized queries only — no string interpolation for SQL. (2) Add sqlx compile-time query checking (sqlx::query! macro) for Rust services. (3) Validate all user inputs against strict schemas before query construction (UUID format for IDs, alphanumeric for barcodes, bounded lengths for search strings). (4) Add integration tests that attempt SQL injection payloads on all endpoints accepting user input.

findings.title: Stripe webhook endpoint lacks signature verification task details
findings.description: Task 4003 mentions 'verify Stripe webhook signature using the webhook secret' but this is a single line in a larger task. Webhook signature verification is critical — if implemented incorrectly or skipped, an attacker could forge payment success events to mark invoices as paid without actual payment. The task also lacks mention of replay attack protection (checking Stripe-Signature timestamp tolerance).
findings.recommendation: Elevate webhook security to a dedicated subtask or add explicit details: (1) Use stripe-rust's built-in webhook::Webhook::construct_event with the webhook secret for signature verification. (2) Implement timestamp tolerance checking (reject events older than 5 minutes). (3) Return 400 for invalid signatures (not 200, which would tell Stripe to stop retrying). (4) Log all webhook signature verification failures as security events. (5) Add negative tests: verify that a forged webhook payload with an invalid signature is rejected.

findings.title: Secrets stored with placeholder values and no encryption verification for etcd
findings.description: Task 1006 creates Kubernetes Secrets with placeholder values and mentions 'optionally create sealed-secrets or external-secrets template for production rotation.' This optional approach means secrets could remain as base64-encoded plaintext in etcd if encryption at rest is not enabled. Additionally, six different secrets with API keys are created in a single namespace without mention of verifying etcd encryption or implementing secret scanning in CI/CD.
findings.recommendation: Make encryption mandatory, not optional: (1) Add a task to verify etcd encryption at rest is enabled on the cluster (EncryptionConfiguration). (2) Mandate External Secrets Operator or Sealed Secrets from the start — not as an optional future step. (3) Add a CI/CD pipeline check that prevents committing unencrypted Secret manifests to the repository. (4) Implement secret scanning in the CI pipeline (e.g., gitleaks, trufflehog) to prevent accidental credential commits.

findings.title: Container images not scanned for vulnerabilities
findings.description: Multiple tasks create Dockerfiles (2001, 3001, 4001) and deploy community container images (Signal-CLI - bbernhard/signal-cli-rest-api in task 1005, Redis 7.2-alpine in task 1003). There are no tasks for container image vulnerability scanning, no mention of image signing or verification, and no policy enforcement to block deployment of images with critical CVEs.
findings.recommendation: Add tasks for: (1) Integrate a container image scanner (Trivy, Grype, or Snyk) into the CI/CD pipeline to scan all built images before pushing to registry. (2) Scan third-party images (signal-cli-rest-api, redis:7.2-alpine) for known CVEs before deployment. (3) Pin all base image digests (not just tags) in Dockerfiles to prevent supply chain attacks. (4) Optionally deploy a Kubernetes admission controller (e.g., Kyverno, OPA Gatekeeper) to block deployment of images with critical/high CVEs.

findings.title: Morgan AI agent has unrestricted access to all backend services
findings.description: Task 7001 configures Morgan with MCP tools that can invoke sigma1_create_invoice, sigma1_finance_report, sigma1_vet_customer, sigma1_social_publish, and other sensitive operations. Task 7006 mentions 'role-based access control within conversation context' but this is implemented at the conversation layer, not at the API layer. If the MCP tool credentials give Morgan's service account full access to all backends, a prompt injection attack or compromised conversation could trigger unauthorized financial operations.
findings.recommendation: Implement defense-in-depth for Morgan's API access: (1) Create separate service tokens with scoped permissions for different tool categories (read-only for catalog, write for quotes, admin for finance reports). (2) Implement a confirmation/approval step for high-impact operations (invoice creation, publishing) that requires explicit human approval even when triggered by Morgan. (3) Add rate limiting on sensitive MCP tool invocations (e.g., max 10 invoices per hour). (4) Log all MCP tool invocations with the conversation context for audit. (5) Implement prompt injection detection/filtering on inbound messages before they reach the LLM.

findings.title: File upload endpoint lacks comprehensive security controls
findings.description: Task 6002 implements photo upload (POST /api/v1/social/upload) with basic file type validation (JPEG, PNG, WEBP) and size limits (20MB). However, there's no mention of: validating file content matches the declared MIME type (magic byte checking), stripping EXIF metadata that may contain GPS coordinates or device info, scanning for embedded malware, or preventing zip-bomb style attacks with compressed images.
findings.recommendation: Enhance file upload security: (1) Validate file magic bytes match declared MIME type — don't rely solely on file extension. (2) Strip EXIF metadata from uploaded images to remove potential PII (GPS coordinates, device info, timestamps). (3) Re-encode images through an image processing library (sharp/libvips) before storage — this neutralizes any embedded payloads. (4) Set Content-Disposition headers on served images to prevent browser execution. (5) Implement antivirus scanning for uploads in production.

findings.title: Signal-CLI pod stores registration state without adequate protection
findings.description: Task 1005 deploys Signal-CLI with a PersistentVolumeClaim storing registration state (~/.local/share/signal-cli). This directory contains the Signal private keys and identity. If the PVC is not encrypted or access-controlled, an attacker gaining access to the storage could impersonate Morgan's Signal identity, read message history, and potentially intercept customer communications.
findings.recommendation: Secure Signal-CLI identity data: (1) Ensure the PVC uses an encrypted storage class. (2) Apply a restrictive SecurityContext on the Signal-CLI pod (runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem where possible, no privilege escalation). (3) Limit network access to the Signal-CLI pod via NetworkPolicy — only the Morgan agent pod should be able to reach it. (4) Implement monitoring/alerting for unexpected access to the Signal-CLI REST API. (5) Document the Signal number registration as a critical security asset with recovery procedures.

findings.title: Dependency versions not pinned and no audit tasks defined
findings.description: Task descriptions mention adding dependencies (axum 0.7, tokio, sqlx, redis, stripe-rust, @aws-sdk/client-s3, effect 3.x, elysia 1.x) but none specify exact version pins with lockfiles, and there are no tasks for running dependency security audits (cargo audit for Rust, npm audit for Node.js, govulncheck for Go).
findings.recommendation: Add dependency security tasks: (1) Pin all dependency versions in lock files (Cargo.lock, bun.lockb/package-lock.json, go.sum) and commit them to version control. (2) Add CI/CD steps running `cargo audit`, `bun audit`/`npm audit`, and `govulncheck` on every PR. (3) Configure Dependabot or Renovate for automated dependency update PRs. (4) Establish a policy for addressing critical/high severity dependency vulnerabilities within 48 hours.

findings.title: Social media OAuth tokens stored without encryption specification
findings.description: Decision point dp-17 in Task 6 asks how to manage Instagram, LinkedIn, and Facebook API tokens but none of the options are resolved. Task 6006, 6007, 6008 each mention managing OAuth tokens but don't specify encryption for stored tokens. Long-lived social media OAuth tokens are high-value targets — a breach could allow an attacker to post content under the company's social media accounts.
findings.recommendation: Resolve dp-17 with a secure approach: (1) Store OAuth tokens encrypted at rest (either via application-level encryption before database storage, or in a dedicated secret store via External Secrets Operator). (2) Implement token scope minimization — request only the minimum OAuth scopes needed for publishing. (3) Implement token revocation capability — ability to immediately revoke all social media tokens from a single admin action. (4) Monitor for token usage anomalies (publishing outside business hours, unusual platforms). (5) Log all token refreshes and social media API calls.

findings.title: Pod security standards not enforced
findings.description: No tasks mention implementing Pod Security Standards (PSS) or Pod Security Admission (PSA). Without these, containers could run as root, mount the host filesystem, use privileged mode, or escalate privileges. The Kubernetes namespace setup (Task 1001) creates namespaces but doesn't apply pod security labels.
findings.recommendation: Add a task to enforce Pod Security Standards: (1) Apply `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: restricted` labels to all application namespaces (sigma1, openclaw, social, web). (2) Apply `pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce: baseline` to the databases namespace (operators may need additional privileges). (3) Ensure all Deployment manifests specify: runAsNonRoot: true, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true (where possible), drop all capabilities. (4) Test that pods violating the policy are rejected.

findings.title: No security event alerting for application-layer attacks
findings.description: While tasks 10003 and 10004 cover Kubernetes API audit logging and Loki integration, there are no tasks for application-layer security event monitoring — detecting and alerting on brute force attempts, anomalous API usage patterns, mass data exfiltration (e.g., scraping entire catalog), or abuse of the vetting/credit scoring endpoints.
findings.recommendation: Add application-layer security monitoring: (1) Implement rate limiting alerts — notify when any client consistently hits rate limits (potential enumeration/scraping). (2) Add anomaly detection for the vetting endpoint — alert on unusual volume of credit checks (potential abuse). (3) Monitor finance endpoints for unusual patterns (mass invoice creation, large payment amounts). (4) Add a WAF rule set or implement request validation at the ingress layer. (5) Create a security events Grafana dashboard separate from operational dashboards.

findings.title: Web chat WebSocket endpoint lacks origin validation and abuse protection
findings.description: Task 7004 implements a WebSocket server for Morgan's web chat with CORS configuration but doesn't mention WebSocket origin validation, connection rate limiting, or message size limits. An attacker could open thousands of WebSocket connections for DoS, or send oversized messages to exhaust memory.
findings.recommendation: Add WebSocket security controls: (1) Validate the Origin header on WebSocket upgrade requests — only allow connections from the legitimate frontend domain. (2) Implement per-IP connection rate limiting (e.g., max 5 concurrent WebSocket connections per IP). (3) Set maximum message size limits (e.g., 4KB per message). (4) Implement connection idle timeouts (disconnect after 30 minutes of inactivity). (5) Add abuse detection — flag IPs sending messages at an inhuman rate.

findings.title: Database connections may not use TLS
findings.description: Task 1002 provisions PostgreSQL and task 2001/3001/4001/5001/6001 connect to it via connection strings from ConfigMap. There's no explicit mention of requiring TLS for database connections (sslmode=require or verify-full in the PostgreSQL connection string), nor for Redis connections.
findings.recommendation: Enforce TLS for all database connections: (1) Configure CloudNative-PG to require TLS connections (serverTLS in the CR). (2) Include sslmode=verify-full in all PostgreSQL connection strings. (3) Enable TLS on Redis/Valkey connections. (4) Ensure all application connection configurations include TLS parameters. (5) Add a connectivity test that verifies connections are encrypted.

findings.title: S3/R2 product-images bucket configured for public read without access logging
findings.description: Task 1004 configures the product-images bucket with 'public read' access for CDN serving. While this is intentional for serving images, there's no access logging enabled on the bucket to detect unauthorized access patterns or potential data enumeration, and no mention of restricting the public read to only the CDN/Cloudflare IPs.
findings.recommendation: Harden object storage: (1) Enable access logging on both S3/R2 buckets. (2) If using Cloudflare R2, restrict public access to only serve through the Cloudflare CDN (not direct bucket URLs). (3) For the event-photos bucket (private), ensure no misconfiguration could make it public. (4) Implement a bucket policy review as part of the security documentation.

findings.title: Google Calendar service account key stored as Kubernetes secret without rotation plan
findings.description: Task 3008 stores a Google Calendar service account key as a Kubernetes secret. Service account keys do not expire automatically and if leaked, provide persistent access. The secret rotation task (10002) focuses on API keys but doesn't explicitly include Google service account key rotation.
findings.recommendation: Include Google service account key in the secret rotation inventory (Task 10002). Consider using Workload Identity Federation instead of service account keys where possible, which eliminates the need for long-lived keys. If keys must be used, implement a rotation schedule (e.g., every 90 days) with automated key creation and old key deletion.

strengths: Network policies (Task 9005/15) with default-deny posture provide strong network-layer segmentation between services
Comprehensive RBAC implementation (Task 10001) inventories all service accounts and applies least-privilege principles with explicit verification testing
Secret rotation is addressed as a dedicated task (10002) with zero-downtime requirements and explicit testing criteria
Kubernetes API audit logging (Task 10003) with Loki/Grafana integration provides visibility into cluster-level security events
Rate limiting is implemented at the application layer (Tasks 2006) with per-tenant controls using Redis-backed sliding window counters
Stripe webhook signature verification is mentioned in the payment integration task (4003)
Effect.Schema validation in the Social Media Engine (Task 6) provides typed request/response validation reducing data integrity issues
Cloudflare Tunnel approach (Tasks 1007, 9004) avoids exposing cluster IPs publicly, reducing attack surface
Failover testing (Task 9006/16) includes explicit data integrity verification and RTO measurement
summary: The plan has a solid infrastructure security foundation with network policies, RBAC, audit logging, and secret rotation. However, it has critical gaps in application-layer authentication — no service exposes endpoints behind an auth middleware, and the Finance/Vetting services handle highly sensitive data (payment processing, credit scores, payroll) without explicit encryption-at-rest or PII protection controls. The Morgan AI agent's broad access to all backend services via MCP tools, combined with the absence of prompt injection defenses, represents a significant attack surface. Resolving the open security decision points (dp-6, dp-11) and adding authentication middleware tasks before implementation begins is essential to avoid a costly retrofit.
